title: Merck — Transplant — Latest Briefing
html:
What Changed in Merck Transplant — Q1 2026

Generated: 2026-04-26 Quarter covered: January - March 2026 Data sources: Merck Q4 2025 earnings (reported Feb 2026), ZoomRx study archive, competitive intelligence, clinical literature updates Reading time: ~10 minutes

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

  1. Prevymis crossed $978M in FY 2025 and is on track to exceed $1.2B in FY 2026. At the current 25-28% growth rate, Prevymis will be a $1B+ franchise by mid-2026 — a significant milestone for what was once a niche HSCT product.
  2. No new ZoomRx deliverables this quarter — the window to propose Wave 2 and new research vectors is now open. The Message Recall Study Wave 1 report was delivered in Q4 2025. Merck should be digesting the messaging gap findings and deciding on next steps. Vinoth should proactively propose Wave 2 tracking + institutional formulary research.
  3. Updated ASTCT guidelines (2025) reinforce Prevymis as standard of care for CMV-seropositive HSCT recipients. The American Society for Transplantation and Cellular Therapy published updated CMV management guidelines, further cementing Prevymis in the HSCT standard of care and providing additional promotional leverage.
  4. Cost-effectiveness literature emerging — mixed signals. Recent analyses suggest Prevymis is most cost-effective in patients with baseline leukopenia or who develop leukopenia during valganciclovir. This nuanced positioning could evolve the value proposition from "better for all D+/R-" to "essential for myelosuppression-prone patients."
  5. Merck Q1 2026 earnings expected late April 2026 — this will be the first readout confirming whether the Q4 2025 momentum (+28%) sustained. Key watch: any Prevymis-specific guidance or commentary on KT adoption trajectory.

2. Data Events That Matter

ASTCT Updated CMV Guidelines — Prevymis Reinforced

  • What happened: The American Society for Transplantation and Cellular Therapy published updated guidelines for CMV prevention and management in HCT recipients (Series #11 update, 2025). Prevymis is recommended for CMV-seropositive adult allogeneic HSCT recipients.
  • What it means: Guideline endorsement is a critical promotional lever for the HSCT indication. The ASTCT recommendation (HSCT6 in the message recall study) had above-average recall in ZoomRx data and serves as a credibility anchor when reps discuss efficacy. For KT, transplant society guideline updates will be the next catalyst — watch for AST (American Society of Transplantation) position statements on letermovir in kidney transplant.
  • What Vinoth should say: "Guideline endorsements are the best promotional support a branded drug can get — they shift the burden of proof from 'why should I prescribe this' to 'why aren't you prescribing this.' ASTCT has done this for HSCT. The key question is when AST will formalize the same for KT."

Cost-Effectiveness Evidence Evolving

  • What happened: Several 2025 publications examined letermovir vs. valganciclovir cost-effectiveness. Key finding: letermovir may be most cost-effective when used among patients with baseline leukopenia or who develop leukopenia during valganciclovir treatment. The 40x+ price differential makes universal cost-effectiveness challenging, but the myelosuppression-prone subpopulation shows clear value.
  • What it means: The value proposition may need to evolve from "Prevymis for all D+/R- patients" to a more targeted "Prevymis is essential for myelosuppression-prone patients and preferred for others." This could actually help adoption — giving P&T committees a clear, defensible criterion for formulary inclusion rather than an all-or-nothing decision.
  • What Vinoth should say: "The cost-effectiveness data actually helps Merck, not hurts them. It gives formulary committees a framework: start with valganciclovir-intolerant or leukopenia-prone patients (an easy 'yes'), then expand. A 'foot in the door' formulary strategy beats an 'all-or-nothing' one."

Competitive Landscape — Stable

  • Livtencity (maribavir, Takeda): No new prophylaxis-relevant developments. Remains positioned for refractory/resistant CMV treatment only. Japan approval (2024) and China approval (2023) expanding global footprint but not changing the competitive dynamic in prophylaxis.
  • Valganciclovir: Remains the price anchor. No new generics or reformulations that would change the cost equation.
  • Pipeline: No Phase 3 CMV prophylaxis competitors identified. Prevymis's first-in-class terminase inhibitor mechanism remains uncontested.

3. Commercial Pulse

Metric Q4 2025 (actual) Q1 2026 (estimate) Commentary
Prevymis Revenue $275M ~$250-260M (est.) Seasonal Q1 softness typical; still on $1B+ annual pace
YoY Growth +28% TBD (expect 20-25%) KT adoption curve still accelerating
KT Adoption Signal Strong Watch for plateau US transplant volumes at record highs
Key Q1 Event — ASTCT guidelines, Q1 earnings upcoming —

4. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q1 2026

Company Brand Q1 2026 Revenue (est.) Key Signal
Takeda Livtencity (maribavir) ~$35M Stable; complementary positioning (treatment failure, not prophylaxis)
Prevymis ~$250-260M (est.) On track for $1B+ annual; 20-25% YoY growth expected

Takeda — Livtencity (~$35M): Livtencity revenue is stable at ~$35M/quarter with no meaningful change in competitive positioning. Takeda expanded Livtencity's geographic footprint (Japan 2024, China 2023) but has not pursued prophylaxis indications. The Prevymis-Livtencity relationship remains complementary: Prevymis prevents CMV reactivation, Livtencity treats breakthrough cases where prophylaxis fails. No Phase 3 CMV prophylaxis competitors have been identified in any pipeline — Prevymis's first-in-class terminase inhibitor mechanism is uncontested. The competitive dynamics that matter for Prevymis are (1) generic valganciclovir on cost, (2) institutional formulary access decisions, and (3) new indication expansion (kidney transplant adoption trajectory). None of these involve branded competitor products.

5. ZoomRx Engagement Health

Study Status Vinoth Action Needed
Message Recall Study Wave 1 Delivered Q4 2025 Follow up on Merck's response to messaging gap findings
Wave 2 Message Recall Not yet proposed Propose now — natural follow-up to track messaging improvements
KT ATU Wave 3 Not yet proposed Recommend H2 2026 fielding to track adoption trajectory
Institutional formulary study Not yet proposed New research vector — addresses the formulary access gap identified in synthesis

Research Pipeline Recommendations

Priority 1 — Wave 2 Message Recall (propose Q2 2026): Track whether Merck acted on the KT7 delivery gap. Has recall improved? Has prescribing intent increased? This is a natural upsell from Wave 1 with clear ROI framing.

Priority 2 — Institutional Formulary Tracking (propose Q2 2026): 20-30 top US transplant centers. Quarterly monitoring of Prevymis formulary status, restriction level, and protocol integration. This fills the biggest research gap — no current study captures the institutional adoption layer that determines whether HCPs CAN prescribe.

Priority 3 — KT ATU Wave 3 (propose Q3 2026 for H2 fielding): Longitudinal tracking of awareness, trial, and usage. Design for cohort-based analysis to distinguish "growing adoption" from "stalled at early adopters."

6. Thesis Updates

Thesis Confidence Change Rationale
KT takes Prevymis past $1.5B by 2027 Slightly strengthened $978M in 2025 + 25-28% growth = $1.2B+ in 2026. $1.5B by 2027 requires ~20% growth from there — achievable if EU ramp continues
Most effective message is least delivered Unchanged (High) No new data since Wave 1. Waiting for Merck's response and Wave 2
R+ patients as growth ceiling Unchanged (Medium) No pipeline signals. Monitor quarterly
Nephrologists as under-served lever Unchanged (Medium) Would strengthen if Wave 2 includes nephrology sub-analysis

7. What's Coming Next Quarter

  • Merck Q1 2026 earnings (late April 2026) — first 2026 Prevymis revenue data point. Watch for: (1) whether 25%+ growth maintained, (2) any Prevymis-specific commentary, (3) any mention of additional indication plans
  • Wave 2 proposal decision — Vinoth should have Merck's response to Wave 1 findings and a clear Wave 2 proposal in hand by end of May
  • Institutional research concept validation — socialize the formulary tracking idea with Merck stakeholders during next account interaction
  • Competitive watch: Livtencity 2025 revenue data (Takeda earnings); any CMV pipeline Phase 2/3 starts
